name.text: Session 03 Final Practice Quiz — Data Categories and Analysis

intro.text: This practice quiz contains 5 short-answer and 2 extended-answer questions. It is designed to consolidate understanding through written reflection and application. Time: ~45 minutes. Total: 100 points. Passing: 60 points.

questiontext.text: (10 points) Explain the Pyramid of Pain concept and describe why detecting adversary TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures) is more valuable than detecting file hashes. Answer in 2–4 sentences.

graderinfo.text:
Key points to look for (award up to 10 pts):

  • Correctly identifies the Pyramid of Pain as a model showing cost to adversary when each IOC type is detected/blocked (2pts)
  • Explains that file hashes are trivially changed (recompile, repack, XOR encrypt) — bottom of pyramid (2pts)
  • Explains that TTPs describe the adversary's behavior and methodology — hardest to change (3pts)
  • Provides a concrete example or clear implication (e.g., detecting PowerShell encoding technique catches the TTP regardless of which hash the malware has) (3pts)

questiontext.text: (10 points) A Logstash pipeline receives a Windows Sysmon Event ID 1 (process creation) log. The timestamp field is UtcTime = 03/15/2024 14:23:01.843. Describe the Logstash filter configuration needed to correctly set the @timestamp field. Answer in 2–4 sentences, and include the relevant Logstash configuration snippet.

graderinfo.text:
Key points:

  • Identifies the correct filter: date filter (3pts)
  • Correct match format string: MM/dd/yyyy HH:mm:ss.SSS (3pts)
  • Sets target to @timestamp (2pts)
  • Mentions timezone: UTC (1pt)
  • Explains WHY this matters (prevents using Logstash receipt time as the event time) (1pt)

name.text: SA-3 — Tactical vs Strategic Threat Intelligence

questiontext.text: (10 points) Explain the difference between tactical threat intelligence and strategic threat intelligence, and give one example of how each is consumed in a SOC. Answer in 2–4 sentences.

graderinfo.text:
Key points:

  • Tactical = machine-readable, short lifespan IOCs (IPs, hashes, Snort rules) (3pts)
  • Tactical example: firewall blocklist auto-updated from MISP feed (2pts)
  • Strategic = high-level narrative, long lifespan, for decision-makers (3pts)
  • Strategic example: threat landscape report to CISO, budget justification (2pts)

name.text: SA-4 — GDPR Data Minimization in SOC

questiontext.text: (10 points) What is GDPR data minimization and how does it affect log retention policy in a SOC? Answer in 2–4 sentences.

graderinfo.text:
Key points:

  • Correctly defines data minimization: only retain personal data as long as necessary (3pts)
  • Identifies that logs contain personal data: usernames, IPs, email addresses (2pts)
  • Explains the conflict with security retention (security wants long retention; GDPR wants minimization) (2pts)
  • Mentions a practical solution: pseudonymization/anonymization after security retention window (3pts)

questiontext.text: (10 points) Describe what alert fatigue is, explain why it is dangerous in a SOC, and provide two concrete strategies to reduce it. Answer in 3–5 sentences.

graderinfo.text:
Key points:

  • Correctly defines alert fatigue: overwhelm from high volume of alerts, especially false positives (2pts)
  • Explains the danger: real threats missed when buried in noise (3pts)
  • Strategy 1 (any valid): rule tuning, exclusions for known FP sources, regular FP rate review (2.5pts)
  • Strategy 2 (any valid): risk-based alerting, severity tiering, SOAR automation, analyst rotation, alert prioritization (2.5pts)

questiontext.text:
(25 points) A healthcare organization has deployed a SIEM and wants to write a detection rule to identify credential dumping attacks (MITRE T1003). The organization uses Active Directory with 500 employees on Windows 10/11 workstations and Windows Server 2019.

Design a comprehensive detection strategy. Your answer must include:

  1. Which log sources to monitor and what specific events or patterns to look for
  2. A sample Splunk SPL or Sigma rule for at least one detection method
  3. Known limitations and potential false positives
  4. Why detecting this technique is particularly critical for a healthcare organization

Answer in 300–500 words.

graderinfo.text:
Grading rubric (25 pts total):

  • Log sources (6pts): Must mention Sysmon Event 10 (lsass access) and at least one of: Event 4688 process creation, Event 4656/4663 object access to SAM/NTDS.dit, network lateral movement events after dumping
  • SPL/Sigma rule (8pts): Must include: EventCode 10 or 4688 with lsass/SAM, filter for suspicious access rights or command-line args, exclusion for legitimate AV processes, MITRE field. Award partial credit for correct structure with minor errors.
  • Limitations/FPs (5pts): Must mention AV products accessing lsass (Defender/MsMpEng), backup solutions, exclusion maintenance burden. At least 2 concrete examples.
  • Healthcare context (6pts): Must connect to PHI/HIPAA requirements, healthcare as ransomware target, potential for patient harm from clinical system disruption, or HIPAA regulatory consequences.

questiontext.text:
(25 points) Explain the threat intelligence lifecycle and describe how a SOC would operationalize threat intelligence from a MISP feed. Your answer should cover the full lifecycle from data collection to detection output, and include at least one concrete example using a real threat actor or malware family (e.g., Emotet, Cobalt Strike, LockBit).

Answer in 300–500 words.

graderinfo.text:
Grading rubric (25 pts total):

  • Lifecycle phases (10pts): Must cover all 6 phases: Planning/Direction, Collection, Processing, Analysis, Dissemination, Feedback. Award 1.5-2pts per phase if explained with some depth.
  • MISP-specific operationalization (8pts): Must describe: how MISP receives feeds, how IOCs are pushed to detection tools (API/lookup), integration with SIEM for alerting. Partial credit for describing the concept without MISP specifics.
  • Concrete example (5pts): Must name a specific threat actor or malware family and describe specific IOCs or TTPs associated with them being tracked and operationalized. Generic examples without specifics receive 2-3pts.
  • Quality and coherence (2pts): Answer flows logically, demonstrates understanding of the process as iterative/continuous rather than one-time.
